sentences.text: Yeah.
The system was-- subject were completely differently taught than in Romania, certainly.
And then we had to study Russian, Russian literature, Russian language.
What-- the point I mostly disliked about school was that the headmaster wanted very often to encourage us to talk about our parents-- what they do at home, if they speak against the-- the system.
That, in other words, they wanted us to [? dop ?] in the-- our parents.
We took it all with a lot of resentment, certainly that nobody would speak against one's parents and so on.
But at the-- I think that the second half of 1940, they started to deport people from Czernowitz.
We were in Czernowitz.
The mood in the Jewish community was one of rising apprehension.
But as I said before, there is not much that we can-- could have done.
The-- the-- we knew that Hitler is approaching fast, because after Austria came Czechloslovakia, and then came Poland.
And we feared what is going to be the-- the future.
But there was nowhere to go.
The big world did not want Jewish refugees, not England, not France.
You couldn't go anywhere.
Their visas were refused.
So all we were like trapped animals in a-- in a tub.
I must confess that nobody knew the full extent of what Hitler is doing.
I suppose that the biggest annihilation started which-- later, too, but it was enough we knew what had happened in Vienna, we knew what had happened in Prague.

And-- but as I said before, there was some sort of a-- a passivity that was born out of the political situation that we didn't have a way out, or were as if trapped in that point-- in-- in that place, and there was no way out.
Yes.
We were afraid of.
We didn't believe.
We were afraid of, that the-- the nightmare comes closer and closer.
Yes.
Yes.

That was more on the political arena, and they felt if all those right wingers, or those anti-semites felt that if their [? theory ?] is finally getting a lot-- a lot of support, because the-- the mightier Hitler became, the mightier they were, and nobody would have dared to open a mouth against them.
Well--
Life was-- as I said, we were not allowed to speak on the street, which most people spoke German didn't speak Yiddish.
There were certain restrictions.
I-- my father, particular, was harassed because he was a Polish citizen and not a Romanian citizen, and therefore, he was, several times, harassed.
They wanted to deport him with the family.
But as usually in Romania, in Romania you could buy yourself out your way.
You can give-- you can bribe yourself out.
The Romanians were always amenable to money.
I think most people amenable to money, but they particularly.
And you could bribe yourself out in the-- as much as possible.
So my father stayed.
But there was long years of suffering in all that.
Pay his way.
We always knew-- we always knew we have to have money.
We cannot spend too much because you always have to have money to save yourself.
I think that one of the reasons that we were so much-- so often accused that Jews are rich or so on is not by the quantities that they really have.
It is because Jews always being chased from place to place, from ghetto to ghetto, had always to have a little bit of gold put aside in order to buy themself out.
The same way that also the ghettos of Germany, they sometimes could buy themself out, not often.
That was already tradition to think always for a rainy day, or what tomorrow will bring, and have something beside to buy your-- your life out.
[SIGH] It was a very, very painful state.
When they-- first of all, that when they came in, they-- pardon me-- they nationalized all private property.

That means my father had acquired several buildings and so on, that all was taking off, that was nationalized, because you couldn't have personal [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] goods-- building or something like that.
My father got a heart attack and was long, long weeks was sick because of the situation.
Our whole life was destabilized because of Communism and how do you adapt yourself to a new surrounding, a new political system that was always on guard of destabilizing us.
You see, they always-- everyone seemed to have been enlisted to tell on the other by the-- by the security.
Basically, not going into too many details, it-- it was a very unsettling time.
We were only a year under the Soviets, because eight days after the beginning of World War II, they retreated and we had German occupation.
But our luck is that it was not German occupation, it was Romanian [?
German. ?] The Germans went further into Russia, in the Romanians stayed and took back [INAUDIBLE] that they a year before had to give up for the Russians.
And they took back their administration.
If it wouldn't have been their administration, I wouldn't be alive today to-- to talk.
Yes.
I had the-- the awful-- the awful privilege of seeing those murderers come again.
Yes.
They were very well organized.
They were very well-- even in retreat.
You couldn't believe that was the army in retreat.
They were everything, the famous German orderliness.
And organization was fully intact.
They were [? awesome ?] and [? frightening. ?]
[DRINKS] It was awful.
It was awful.
We knew somehow that from now on, our life is a-- a very big question mark.
And everybody just took it-- took life-- there-- there was no-- look, there was no choice in what we could do.
So we had to take it.
We always would pray and hope.
Well, just-- if you-- the first thing-- I do not remember exactly, chronologically, what came first.
But one of the first thing that they did was that every Jew had to wear a yellow star.
It was-- I do not remember the exact measurements.
I think three centimeter by three centimeter or six centimeter-- I can't remember-- on the left-hand side.
So when you went out, the first they-- they-- the first I think was that we could only go out from our homes for two hours in the morning, from 10:00 to 12:00, with the star, and then stay at home.
Then, I do not remember if it was in August or in September that the ghetto was-- came to order, that all the Jews were [? and ?] [? where ?] should go to ghetto.
And then that was closed with-- with timber boards.
It was closed.
And there was one entrance only to the ghetto.
Were you forced out of your home?
Yes.
No, no, no.
Our home was part of the ghetto.
I-- we were not forced out.
Because our home was part of the ghetto.
We only left later on, our home in order--
In-- in the ghetto.
Our home was part of the ghetto.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I remember that.
Our home was part of the ghetto.
But there's so many people coming in, all-- all my mother's family that were-- that their places were non-ghetto now, came to live with us.
It-- it was a lot of people had-- had to come in.
We ended up being maybe 10 or 16 people in a-- in a room, because of all the-- all the people being just crowded together.
Food.
There was a market which the Romanians came in with their goods.
They did not want any money.
They wanted good-- goods.
They exchanged.
There was a exchange.
If you gave them a pillow, then you would get a piece of butter.
Or you gave them an item of clothing, or something from the home, or a rug, or a coat, or-- I don't remember.
I remember always my mother taking an item and going and buying something for that item.
No.
They did-- they were not interested in money.
They wanted-- because, you see, they wanted to get all those goods for a minimum price.
And they were not interested in money.
[DRINKS]
Well, the ghetto-- I cannot remember the exact months in which the ghetto was established.

And then they started to make the ghetto smaller, in taking all those that lived in the peripheral-- the peripheral in the ghetto-- streets, they took away to a place called Transnistria, that was over the Dniester.
The Dniester is-- is the river that is the border between Romania-- that was the border, until 1940, between Romania and Russia.
It's a big river.
And on the other side was desolation.
And people just died from hunger, and from beatings, and from shootings, and from drownings, and-- and so on and so forth.
From the Jewish population that we had in Czernowitz, I think that 30,000 eventually survived.
I think there were 120,000 or 100,000.
I don't remember exactly.
But a third survived, which is comparative to Poland.
A-- a very good percentage still.
The Germans pressed on the Romanians to start with the extermination of the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They tried to find a [? modus ?] how to do it and still not to do it the German way-- how, as Germany had called it, extermination camps.
I do not know their mentality, why and what.
I just know that after several deportations, they stopped.
The Romanians stopped.

It was rumored in the ghetto that they got a very high amount of money in Bucharest, which is the capital city of Romania-- they got a high amount of money as a bribe, in order to stop the annihilation.
I do not know as much as that is truth or not or if it was only rumor.
That--
I think after two or three months.
It was winter that-- we are talking about 1941.
In spring 1942, the thing starts again.
We were still--
--in the ghetto.
Yes.
When the second wave-- we called it the wave-- the second wave of deportation started, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were very fearful, certainly.

And my father first bought himself a certificate that he's not-- because he has a heart condition-- that he is not transportable, that he cannot go on a transport, which was, for the money that it costs, it was really not very much.

But we couldn't get any other-- What I didn't say before is that the Romanian administration, when they came back, in order to establish themself again in Czernowitz, they needed people who knew how to go along, how-- how to administer, and how to make things work.
So they gave certain people that were necessary for them, they gave them exemptions.
Some, even, that they needed badly, were even exempt of being in the-- in the ghetto.
I had an uncle who was very high functional in the-- the railway.
He really knew his job.
And he got a exemption.
And he had even allowed-- was allowed to be out of the ghetto and to go back to his house.
We couldn't buy ourself something like that.
So the best my father could do was buy himself that he's not transferable.
So when the next wave started, we had once that they came up to our place and told us to go out.
I showed them the-- the certificate.
They looked at my father.
My father was really, very sick.
So they didn't take us away, which was great, great luck that they still acknowledged that piece of paper.
But we knew that the second time, we wouldn't get away with it.
No.
No.
They were Romanians.
As far as I can remember-- memory dims after so many years-- there was always somebody from the security.
There were two soldiers.
There was a policeman.
There was a group that came and they just told you all you can-- could take was a rucksack, a knapsack, with just a few things.
And then you were made to walk a long, quite a long distance, the group getting bigger and bigger and bigger.
And then they took to an assembly platz-- place-- that was usually on a-- on a soccer field, on a place where in the vicinity of the railway.
And the people were all put on the railway and off over the Dniester.
After that incident, well, I can remember that we were extremely fearful that the next time, we will be taken.

And there was a group in Czernowitz who organized-- that means, to organize means to give money, in Romania-- a-- a-- a certain lawyer, Romanian lawyer, was sent to Bucharest to try to find some way of assuring the security of all those Polish citizens.
In-- when the war-- when the-- when there was the-- the agreement between Russia and Germany to divide Poland, the Polish official government fled to Romania.
On their way to Bucharest, they had to pass Czernowitz.
So that means that they were, for maybe a few days, in Czernowitz.
And they rested.
And then they continued to Bucharest.
In Romania, they established a provisional government-- Polish government.
Now, the trick was that this government was under the-- I forgot how you say it in English.
In Hebrew, it's [HEBREW].
That is, they-- they were under protection-- I suppose would be the nearest word-- under the protection of the Swiss government that protected all those Polish citizens, officials, that fled.
What we really aimed to do is to get the same documents from the Swiss consul that we are protected by them.
Because of my father being Polish.
He-- we had a Polish passport, Polish-- my father had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where he was born--
Birth certificate.
And-- and really, he came back that-- the lawyer came back.
And the sum of money was quite high.
And my mother was very concerned.
She said, how many times can we shell out that?
Maybe we shouldn't do it, because who knows what is going to come.
But I can remember, I was very young.
And I approached my father.
And I pummeled his chest.
And I screamed, I want to live.
I want to live.
You have to do it.
You have to give.
I don't care what's going to be later.
But if there is a way out now, to save us one deportation, two deportations, we have to do it.
My father started to cry because he saw me screaming, I want to live.
And he decided he's going to go-- get-- go through with it.
He gave the money.
And the-- after a week or two, we got the-- the passport with a very big red stamp.
Because for a Romanian to see a big stamp, that is a sign of authority.
And that was-- I do not know what-- how-- what time passed until there was another wave of deportation.
And again, they came.
And I showed them the passport.
And I showed them.
And they said, look.
We have got instructions not to recognize that.
We have to take you.
If you will come to the assembly place, if you will do-- if you will be able to do something there, I don't know.
But we have to take you.
So, that means taking that-- the first thing, giving my father the-- the-- the nitroglycerine under the tongue, because of his heart condition.
And we started marching to that assembly place.
When we arrived there, it was a huge, huge place.
And all the moaning and wailing and crying of all those people was really heartbreaking.
The prime minister, the main ruler of Romania at that time, was a little fellow, was that small, fat.
Called [?
Mironescu. ?] He was, afterwards, I think he-- what-- accused and he was sentenced to death when the Russians came back.
And he arrived there.
I had the luck that one of the soldiers that was my-- that was supposed to guard me-- not guard me-- that I shouldn't escape-- was most probably a nice fellow.
I talked very good Romanian.
And I explained to him that I have to do something, because I'm unjustifiably there.
We are-- that is a big-- we are not Romanian citizens and they [? don't ?] have the right to take us.
Well, he was-- he was good in that respect.
So I started going with him and trying to organize-- there were-- I saw quite a few people that were the-- in the same state, [INAUDIBLE] they had this passport.
So I organized-- I was a little ball of energy.
I was 15 and 1/2. 15 and 1/2.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Organize that we should be in group together, that not each and every one should have to fight for himself.
And when those-- we were already several families, one of the-- one of the men saw somebody passing-- a non-Jew, certainly.
And he gave him money, he should go immediately into town to alert that particular lawyer that was the [? organizer. ?] After one or two hours, he came.
In the meantime, we saw--
The lawyer arrived.
And he later had a talk with [?
Mironescu, ?] who was the-- the super, super chief.
And he got an absolvence, at that time, that we can go home.
That was miracle of miracles.
I couldn't believe my ears that-- that he said that we are free.
Now, that was out of town.
Because it was a soccer field out of town.
And my father, with his condition, could barely walk back from there.
So what we-- I did was I saw a peasant in those peasant carriage-- I do not know if I can say the carriage.
It is a-- a-- a-- one horse, just carrying that.
I don't know how you say it in English.
Anyhow, I put my father there, into that, my mother.
And off we went towards Czernowitz.
We went, not much then, maybe 15 minutes or 20 minutes.
Somebody approached us, stopped us, and said to that peasant, what are you doing?
You are taking Jews back to Czernowitz?
What are you doing?
You take them immediately back.
You see, we had to have the yellow stars, so they immediately saw it.
So, we were-- went back on the way, which I saw that that is desperation.
[SIGHS] But, as one can say, there are miracles happening, or whatever.
Suddenly, a policeman was standing there.
And as it so happened, my father knew that policeman because he used to bribe him every Sunday.
My father used to open the shop, so he bribed him that-- because one was not allowed to--
And my father told him, look.
Look what's happening.
And he told him the whole thing.
So that policeman, knowing that he will get another bribe, turned us around.
And he took us home.
Until the home.
When we arrived home-- Home was where?
Home in the ghetto.
In the ghetto.
When we arrived home, nobody could believe that we're alive.
Because nobody ever comes home from-- from that-- from the assembly place.
And when we arrived home, and we sat down, I told my father, that will not-- that is not good.
Because me-- who knows that the next time they come and take us, they will acknowledge that passport.
[SIGHS] My father saw that I was right but he was tired.
But I-- I finally had my way.
And we started looking where we should go into hiding.
My grandfather died two days after the war broke out.
He-- he wasn't-- he wasn't taken anywhere.
He just died.
My mother's sisters went away to-- to Transnistria.
Her stepmother was-- was taken to Transnistria.
She-- her brother, that was later killed by the Germans in the most brutal way, in the most brutal way.
They put a chain around his throat and put a stone on him and threw him in the Dniester.
That is something-- a most brutal way.

The large extended family-- one-- an auntie and an uncle were-- were remained in Czernowitz because they got the-- that approval that the-- the men are necessary for-- for the administration of the city.
So they survived.
The rest isn't there anymore.
